does: This paper identifies and addresses compounding failures in multi-agent systems performing subjective reasoning tasks like persuasion. The authors diagnose semantic leakage in standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) as a root cause of problem drift and sycophantic conformity in multi-agent debate scenarios. They propose Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), which routes retrieval through a discrete categorical taxonomy rather than direct semantic similarity, creating a bottleneck that decouples argumentative structure from topical content. Through zero-shot cross-domain evaluations, they demonstrate that TS-RAG significantly improves abstract logical reasoning transfer and enables lightweight agents to consistently outperform parametrically superior opponents by maintaining strategic integrity across persuasion trajectories, addressing the failure modes that plague existing multi-agent debate approaches in open-ended domains.
connects: This work builds on Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) frameworks that use agent disagreement to improve reasoning in deterministic domains, but extends them to subjective tasks where standard debate breaks down due to conformity pressures and problem drift. It responds to known limitations in Retrieval-Augmented Generation systems where vocabulary overlap can prioritize superficially similar but logically irrelevant information, a problem well-documented in semantic retrieval literature. The paper connects to research on compounding errors in long-horizon agent behavior and builds on abstraction-based retrieval methods that have shown promise in separating high-level reasoning from surface-level content matching. It also engages with work on asymmetric multi-agent interactions and capability transfer in foundation model systems.
enables: This work enables more robust deployment of weaker models in adversarial reasoning tasks by providing a mechanism to preserve strategic coherence despite parameter disadvantages. The taxonomic bottleneck approach could generalize to other domains beyond persuasion where content-structure decoupling matters, such as negotiation, technical problem-solving, and policy reasoning. The paper points toward designing retrieval systems that explicitly represent logical or strategic categories rather than leaning solely on semantic similarity, potentially improving reliability in any task where domain-specific reasoning structures must be preserved. Future work could explore learning taxonomies automatically from task structure, applying TS-RAG to multi-step planning problems, and investigating how categorical routing affects human-AI collaboration in subjective judgment tasks.
